title: Dog Foreign Object Under Eyelid: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn what to do if your dog has a foreign object under their eyelid. Discover why this is a high-priority emergency and how a photo helps vets.
content_html:
TL;DR: A foreign object in a dog's eye is a high-priority emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention to prevent permanent blindness. Prevent your dog from pawing at the eye and contact an emergency vet right away.

What constitutes a foreign object in my dog's eye?

A foreign object (or foreign body) is any external material that becomes lodged under your dog's eyelid or on the surface of the eye. Common examples include grass seeds, thorns, dirt, or small shards of plastic. Because the eye is one of the most sensitive parts of the body, even a microscopic speck can cause intense pain and rapid damage to the ocular tissues.

Is it an emergency if my dog has something stuck in their eye?

  • Urgency Level: High. A foreign object stuck under the eyelid is a critical veterinary emergency.
  • Your dog’s natural instinct to paw at the eye can cause corneal ulcers, punctures, or permanent blindness within hours.
  • Contact an emergency vet immediately to have the object safely removed under professional supervision.

How does taking a photo of my dog's eye help the triage team?

  • If your dog is calm, a clear photo allows the triage team to see the object's position and the degree of swelling.
  • High-resolution images help the veterinarian assess the severity of the injury before you arrive.
  • Providing a photo helps the clinic prepare necessary tools, such as local anesthetics or specialized flushing equipment, in advance.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of a suspected foreign body under the eyelid, presenting signs include blepharospasm, tearing, and potential secondary iridocyclitis. Ophthalmic examination should identify the foreign body on the conjunctival surface or in the posterior third eyelid fornix. Immediate treatment includes removal of the foreign body, often performed under topical anesthesia. The eyelid must be protected from self-trauma using an Elizabethan collar or hard eye cup, and a temporary tarsorrhaphy may be needed to protect the cornea if the blink response is impaired. Postoperative therapy includes topical antibiotics and corticosteroids, as well as systemic antibiotics and NSAIDs.

Chapter: Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1697)

faqs.question: Can I try to remove the object at home?
faqs.answer: No. Attempting to remove an object yourself can cause the dog to flinch, potentially leading to a corneal puncture. Vets often use topical numbing drops or sedation to remove objects safely.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a foreign body in a dog's eye?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include constant squinting, excessive tearing, thick discharge, a red appearance to the white of the eye, and the dog repeatedly pawing at their face.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: If possible, place an Elizabethan collar (cone) on your dog to prevent them from pawing at the eye. Keep the car cool and try to keep your dog as calm as possible.
